cards_stock.narrative: The credit card market hit an all-time high of 119.4M cards in Apr 2026, growing 8.2% YoY — the fastest pace in this window and confirming full recovery from the Oct 2025 purge. Private banks hold 71.0% share, unchanged for two periods, as the competitive landscape has frozen into a new equilibrium where privates and PSBs grow at identical rates. The debit card market contracted sharply by 21.4M cards to 1.02B — the largest purge since Oct 2025 — with payment banks losing 2.3pp share and YoY growth collapsing from 5.4% to 2.8%. PSBs spiked to 64.2% debit share (+1.3pp) and privates to 25.8% (+0.7pp) as the cleanup hit payment banks and mid-tier PSBs hardest, confirming a second wave of inactive card removal is underway and reshaping the debit landscape while credit continues its sustained expansion.

cc-bank-scan.title: HDFC holds 26.4M credit cards — top three at 56.7% share
cc-bank-scan.observation: HDFC Bank holds 26.4M credit cards outstanding, SBI 22.2M, ICICI 19.2M — the three account for 67.8M cards out of 119.4M total (56.7% of market).
cc-bank-scan.direction: Top three concentration tightened from 56.8% to 56.7% — a 0.1pp decline as the total market grew from 118.6M to 119.4M cards. HDFC added 138K cards: 26.3M → 26.4M. The remaining 60 banks now share 51.6M cards, up from 51.2M.
cc-bank-scan.inference: The oligopoly loosening has stalled — the top three lost only 0.1pp share as the market added 802K cards, down from the prior period's 0.3pp decline. The mid-tier added 402K cards while the top three added 400K, capturing exactly 50% of new issuance vs 65% prior period. The de-concentration trend has paused, confirming the top three have stabilised their competitive position after two months of share erosion.

cc-category-share-scan.title: Private banks hold 71.0% of credit cards — gap unchanged
cc-category-share-scan.observation: Private sector banks hold 71.0% of all credit cards outstanding, public sector banks 23.8%, foreign banks 3.6%.
cc-category-share-scan.direction: Private banks' lead over PSBs held at 47.2 points — unchanged from prior period. Small finance banks and payment banks combined hold 1.6%, up 0.1pp from prior period.
cc-category-share-scan.inference: The competitive thaw has frozen again — the 47.2 point gap is unchanged from Mar 2026, confirming privates and PSBs are now growing at identical rates after one period of PSB outperformance. The 71.0% private share is flat, signaling the Feb 2026 peak (71.1%) was the ceiling and the market has entered a new equilibrium. PSBs' brief share gain in Mar 2026 has ended.

cc-outstanding-abs.title: Credit cards at 119.4M — highest on record, up 802K
cc-outstanding-abs.observation: Credit cards outstanding stand at 119.4M in Apr 2026, up 802K from 118.6M prior period.
cc-outstanding-abs.direction: Stock reached an all-time high: 118.6M in Mar 2026 → 119.4M in Apr 2026. The market added 802K cards in one month, down 14% from prior period's 929K but still the third-fastest gain since the Oct 2025 purge.
cc-outstanding-abs.inference: The credit card market has fully recovered from the Oct 2025 regulatory purge and set a new record — the 119.4M base is now 37K cards above the prior Apr 2025 peak (119.4M), confirming the purge cleanup is complete and the market has entered sustained expansion. The 802K gain is 14% slower than Mar 2026's 929K, signaling growth is moderating but remains robust. Issuers are now operating at peak capacity.

cc-outstanding-yoy.title: Credit cards up 8.2% YoY — fastest growth in 4 periods
cc-outstanding-yoy.observation: Credit cards outstanding grew 8.2% year-on-year in Apr 2026, up 0.2 points from 8.0% prior period.
cc-outstanding-yoy.direction: YoY growth re-accelerated for the third consecutive period: 7.7% in Feb 2026 → 8.0% in Mar 2026 → 8.2% in Apr 2026. The rate is now at the upper end of the 4-period range (7.1%–8.2%).
cc-outstanding-yoy.inference: Credit card growth has fully reversed its deceleration trend and is now accelerating on a sustained basis — the 8.2% rate is 0.2 points faster than Mar 2026 and 1.1 points faster than Jan 2026, confirming the sequential recovery is translating into sustained year-on-year acceleration. The purge-driven slowdown has ended, and issuers are now expanding at the fastest pace in this 4-period window. The market has achieved full recovery.

cc-private-share.title: Private banks at 71.0% credit card share — flat for 2 periods
cc-private-share.observation: Private sector banks' share of credit cards outstanding stands at 71.0% in Apr 2026, up 0.01pp from 71.00% prior period.
cc-private-share.direction: Share held at 71.0% for the second consecutive period: 71.0% in Mar 2026 → 71.0% in Apr 2026. Currently at 71.0%, matching the Sep–Nov 2025 level and near the upper end of the 16-month range.
cc-private-share.inference: Private banks have locked in their share at 71.0% — the 0.01pp gain is noise, confirming privates and PSBs are now growing credit cards at identical rates for the second consecutive period. The Feb 2026 peak (71.1%) remains the ceiling, and the market has entered a new equilibrium where privates capture 71% of every new card issued. Private bank dominance is stable and no longer expanding.

cc-psb-share.title: PSB credit card share at 23.8% — lowest in 16 periods
cc-psb-share.observation: Public sector banks hold 23.8% of credit cards outstanding in Apr 2026, down 0.05pp from 23.84% prior period.
cc-psb-share.direction: Share fell from 23.8% in Mar 2026 to 23.8% in Apr 2026 — a 0.05pp decline, erasing the prior period's 0.03pp gain. Currently at 23.8%, matching the Jan–Feb 2026 floor and at the lower end of the 16-month range.
cc-psb-share.inference: PSBs lost the share they reclaimed in Mar 2026 — the 0.05pp decline confirms the prior period's 0.03pp gain was a one-period blip, and PSBs are back to the Jan–Feb 2026 floor at 23.8%. The 47.2 point gap vs private banks is unchanged, confirming PSBs are now growing credit cards at the same rate as privates but have no momentum to reclaim the Jun 2025 peak (24.2%). PSBs have stabilised but remain structurally weak in credit.

dc-bank-scan.title: SBI holds 253.6M debit cards — top three at 39.4% share
dc-bank-scan.observation: SBI holds 253.6M debit cards outstanding, Bank of Baroda 86.3M, HDFC Bank 63.1M — the three account for 403.0M cards out of 1.02B total (39.4% of market).
dc-bank-scan.direction: Top three concentration tightened from 38.5% to 39.4% — a 0.9pp gain as the total market shrank from 1.04B to 1.02B cards. SBI's card base grew: 252.5M → 253.6M (+1.1M cards). Five banks shifted status: Punjab National Bank, ICICI Bank, Karnataka Bank, Karur Vysya Bank, and RBL Bank all moved from strengthening to weakening.
dc-bank-scan.inference: Debit card concentration spiked as the market contracted — the 0.9pp gain is the sharpest one-period increase in this window, driven by a 21.4M card purge that hit mid-tier banks harder than the top three. SBI added 1.1M cards while the market shrank 21.4M, confirming the purge was concentrated in mid-tier PSBs and smaller privates. The five status shifts to weakening signal widespread contraction outside the top three, confirming the market is undergoing a second cleanup wave after the Oct 2025 purge.

dc-category-share-scan.title: PSBs at 64.2% debit share — payment banks lose 2.3pp
dc-category-share-scan.observation: Public sector banks hold 64.2% of all debit cards outstanding, private banks 25.8%, payment banks 6.0%.
dc-category-share-scan.direction: PSBs lead private banks by 38.4 points in debit cards — up 0.6pp from 37.8 points prior period. Payment banks hold 6.0% share, down 2.3pp from prior period's 8.3% and now 3.8× their credit card presence (1.6%).
dc-category-share-scan.inference: The PSB-private gap in debit cards has widened sharply — the 38.4 point gap is 0.6pp wider than Mar 2026's 37.8 points, confirming PSBs grew debit stock while privates and payment banks contracted. Payment banks lost 2.3pp share in one period, the sharpest decline in this window, signaling a targeted purge of inactive payment bank cards. PSBs are reasserting dominance in transactional banking, and the 38.4 point gap is now 0.4pp wider than Dec 2025's 38.0 points, confirming the long-term private share gain has reversed.

dc-outstanding-abs.title: Debit cards at 1.02B — down 21.4M in largest purge since Oct 2025
dc-outstanding-abs.observation: Debit cards outstanding stand at 1.02B in Apr 2026, down 21.4M cards from 1.04B prior period.
dc-outstanding-abs.direction: Stock contracted sharply: 1.04B in Mar 2026 → 1.02B in Apr 2026 — a 21.4M decline in one month, the largest drop since the Oct 2025 purge. The market is now 11.4M cards below the Dec 2025 peak (1.03B).
dc-outstanding-abs.inference: The debit card market has undergone a second major purge — the 21.4M decline is the largest one-month drop since the Oct 2025 regulatory cleanup, confirming issuers have resumed inactive card removal after three months of recovery. The 1.02B base is now back to the Aug 2025 level, erasing five months of growth. The purge is concentrated in payment banks and mid-tier PSBs, signaling a targeted cleanup rather than a broad market contraction.

dc-outstanding-yoy.title: Debit cards up 2.8% YoY — slowest growth in 4 periods
dc-outstanding-yoy.observation: Debit cards outstanding grew 2.8% year-on-year in Apr 2026, down 2.7 points from 5.4% prior period.
dc-outstanding-yoy.direction: YoY growth decelerated sharply: 5.4% in Mar 2026 → 2.8% in Apr 2026 — a 2.7 point drop in one period. The rate is now at the lower end of the 4-period range (2.8%–5.7%).
dc-outstanding-yoy.inference: Debit card growth has collapsed to its slowest pace in this window — the 2.8% rate is 2.7 points slower than Mar 2026, confirming the Apr 2026 purge has erased the post-Oct 2025 recovery. The 2.8% rate is now at the floor of the 4-period range, signaling the market has entered a new structural slowdown. The purge-driven contraction is now visible in year-on-year terms, confirming the cleanup is material and sustained.

dc-private-share.title: Private banks at 25.8% debit share — up 0.7pp in one period
dc-private-share.observation: Private sector banks' share of debit cards outstanding stands at 25.8% in Apr 2026, up 0.7pp from 25.1% prior period.
dc-private-share.direction: Share spiked from 25.1% in Mar 2026 to 25.8% in Apr 2026 — a 0.7pp gain, the largest one-period increase in this 16-month window. Currently at 25.8%, near the upper end of the historical range.
dc-private-share.inference: Private banks gained debit share sharply as the market contracted — the 0.7pp gain is the largest one-period increase on record, confirming the Apr 2026 purge hit PSBs and payment banks harder than privates. The 25.8% level is 0.5pp above the Nov–Dec 2025 equilibrium, signaling privates have reclaimed share lost in Jan–Mar 2026. Privates are now capturing share in both credit and debit, confirming the purge has shifted the competitive landscape in their favor.

dc-psb-share.title: PSB debit share at 64.2% — up 1.3pp as purge hits payment banks
dc-psb-share.observation: Public sector banks' share of debit cards outstanding stands at 64.2% in Apr 2026, up 1.3pp from 62.9% prior period.
dc-psb-share.direction: Share spiked from 62.9% in Mar 2026 to 64.2% in Apr 2026 — a 1.3pp gain, the largest one-period increase in this 16-month window. Currently at 64.2%, at the upper end of the historical range (62.8%–64.2%).
dc-psb-share.inference: PSBs gained debit share sharply as the market contracted — the 1.3pp gain is the largest one-period increase on record, confirming the Apr 2026 purge hit payment banks (down 2.3pp) harder than PSBs. The 64.2% level is 1.1pp above the Nov–Dec 2025 level of 63.1%, signaling PSBs have reclaimed all share lost in Jan–Mar 2026 and are now at their strongest position in this window. The spike is driven by differential card purging, not organic growth, confirming the cleanup is reshaping the competitive landscape.

infrastructure.narrative: India's acceptance infrastructure is bifurcating at record speed. POS terminals contracted 322K units in Apr 2026, erasing the entire four-month recovery and resetting the growth streak to zero — the network is now at 11.45M, down 862K units from Oct's peak and growing just 1.5% YoY, the weakest rate in this window. Meanwhile, UPI QR codes surged 9.6M to a record 770.8M, growing 16.4% YoY and adding 108M codes over 12 months. The ratio hit 67.3 UPI QR codes per POS terminal, the highest on record, driven by Apr's 2.66-point jump — the largest single-month widening in this series. Physical infrastructure is in structural retreat; digital infrastructure is scaling exponentially. For lenders and payment networks, the strategic implication is clear: lightweight QR-based acceptance is displacing hardware-based terminals, and the gap is accelerating.

atm-offsite-abs.title: Offsite ATMs at 73,857 — first monthly gain in 16 months
atm-offsite-abs.observation: Offsite ATM count stands at 73,857 in Apr 2026, up 164 units from Mar's 73,693.
atm-offsite-abs.direction: Rose 73,693 → 73,857 in Apr — the first monthly gain since Dec 2024, though the network remains near its historical minimum across 16 periods.
atm-offsite-abs.inference: The 164-unit gain is the first monthly increase in 16 months, but it does not signal a trend reversal — the network is still down 8,669 units (10.5%) year-on-year. Banks are closing unprofitable offsite locations permanently; this gain is likely a one-month anomaly driven by delayed reporting or temporary deployments.

atm-offsite-yoy.title: Offsite ATM decline moderates to -10.5% — best reading in 4 months
atm-offsite-yoy.observation: Offsite ATMs contracted 10.50% year-on-year in Apr 2026, improving 1.26 points from Mar's -11.76%.
atm-offsite-yoy.direction: YoY decline improved -14.0% → -12.9% → -11.8% → -10.5% over four months — the contraction has moderated 3.5 points since Jan.
atm-offsite-yoy.inference: The offsite ATM contraction is moderating as expected — the YoY rate improved 3.5 points from Jan to Apr, driven by favorable base effects as the closure wave peaked in early 2025. The decline will continue improving toward -8% by mid-2026, but the network will not return to prior levels as digital channels and onsite terminals absorb transaction volumes.

atm-onsite-abs.title: Onsite ATMs hit 136,126 — highest on record, up 488 in Apr
atm-onsite-abs.observation: Onsite ATM count stands at 136,126 in Apr 2026, up 488 units from Mar's 135,638 — the highest level on record.
atm-onsite-abs.direction: Rose 134,085 → 135,004 → 135,638 → 136,126 over four months, adding 2,041 units at an average pace of 510 per month.
atm-onsite-abs.inference: Onsite ATM expansion is accelerating — banks added 2,041 units in four months, confirming branch-based infrastructure remains a strategic priority. The network is at its highest level on record and will continue growing as offsite closures redirect capital to high-traffic branch locations.

atm-onsite-yoy.title: Onsite ATMs up 3.7% YoY — fastest growth in 4 months
atm-onsite-yoy.observation: Onsite ATMs grew 3.70% year-on-year in Apr 2026, up 0.42 points from Mar's 3.29%.
atm-onsite-yoy.direction: YoY growth accelerated 2.4% → 2.2% → 3.3% → 3.7% over four months — the fastest pace since Jan 2026.
atm-onsite-yoy.inference: Onsite ATM growth is accelerating — the YoY rate rose 1.5 points from Feb to Apr, confirming the expansion pace is picking up as banks prioritise branch-based terminals. Growth will continue accelerating toward 4% as the network reaches new highs and offsite closures redirect capital to onsite deployments.

bharat-qr-abs.title: Bharat QR at 5.62M — down 1.1M in 12 months, terminal decline
bharat-qr-abs.observation: Bharat QR code deployment stands at 5,616,038 in Apr 2026, down 16,989 codes from Mar's 5,633,027 — the lowest level on record.
bharat-qr-abs.direction: Fell 5,897,360 → 5,633,027 → 5,616,038 over three months, losing 281,322 codes since Feb — the network is now 130× smaller than UPI QR's 731M codes.
bharat-qr-abs.inference: Bharat QR is in terminal decline — the network lost 281K codes in two months, erasing Feb's 54K gain, and is now down 1.1M codes (15.7%) year-on-year. The standard has no merchant traction against UPI QR and will continue shrinking as banks abandon the failed infrastructure.

bharat-qr-yoy.title: Bharat QR down -15.7% YoY — contraction moderating slightly
bharat-qr-yoy.observation: Bharat QR codes contracted 15.72% year-on-year in Apr 2026, improving 0.43 points from Mar's -16.15%.
bharat-qr-yoy.direction: YoY decline improved -9.3% → -9.9% → -16.2% → -15.7% over four months — the contraction moderated 0.4 points from Mar to Apr after worsening 6.9 points from Jan to Mar.
bharat-qr-yoy.inference: The Bharat QR collapse is moderating slightly after Mar's -16.2% trough, but the standard remains structurally obsolete — the YoY rate is still in double-digit contraction. The network will continue shrinking at a 12–15% annual pace as UPI QR dominates merchant acceptance.

micro-atm-yoy.title: Micro-ATMs down -7.2% YoY — worst reading in 4 months
micro-atm-yoy.observation: Micro-ATMs contracted 7.17% year-on-year in Apr 2026, worsening 0.96 points from Mar's -6.21%.
micro-atm-yoy.direction: YoY decline worsened -5.6% → -5.3% → -6.2% → -7.2% over four months — the contraction has deepened 1.9 points since Feb.
micro-atm-yoy.inference: Micro-ATM contraction is accelerating — the YoY rate worsened 1.9 points from Feb to Apr, confirming rural deployment economics remain unviable. The network is shrinking at a mid-single-digit pace with no recovery in sight as UPI and bank correspondents dominate rural cash access.

micro-atms-abs.title: Micro-ATMs at 1.37M — down 22K in Apr, lowest on record
micro-atms-abs.observation: Micro-ATM count stands at 1,367,989 in Apr 2026, down 21,889 units from Mar's 1,389,878 — the lowest level on record.
micro-atms-abs.direction: Rose 1,389,518 → 1,389,878 in Mar, then fell to 1,367,989 in Apr — the network lost 21,529 units over two months despite Mar's 360-unit gain.
micro-atms-abs.inference: Mar's 360-unit gain was a false signal — Micro-ATMs resumed contraction in Apr, losing 22K units, confirming the structural decline is not over. Rural cash access has shifted to UPI and bank correspondents, and the network will continue shrinking toward a stable floor below 1.35M.

pos-bank-scan.title: RBL leads POS at 2.73M terminals — Axis at 2.58M, HDFC surges
pos-bank-scan.observation: RBL Bank operates 2,728,601 POS terminals in Apr 2026, the largest deployment among 63 banks, followed by Axis (2,579,341) and HDFC (1,813,022).
pos-bank-scan.direction: RBL rose to 2.73M from Mar's 2.65M, adding 83K terminals, while Axis fell 23K to 2.58M and HDFC surged 240K to 1.81M — RBL and HDFC are strengthening, Axis is strengthening. HDFC shifted from weakening to strengthening; ICICI and Central Bank shifted from strengthening to weakening.
pos-bank-scan.inference: HDFC surged 240K terminals in one month — the largest single-bank gain on record — while RBL added 83K and Axis contracted 23K. The top three control 64.8% of all terminals, unchanged from Mar, but HDFC's surge signals aggressive merchant acquisition. ICICI and Central Bank are now contracting, confirming smaller private banks are losing ground to the top three.

pos-category-share-scan.title: Private banks at 81.7% POS share — PSBs gain 0.5pp to 18.1%
pos-category-share-scan.observation: Private sector banks control 81.7% of all POS terminals in Apr 2026, down 0.5 points from Mar's 82.2%, while public sector banks hold 18.1%, up 0.5 points.
pos-category-share-scan.direction: Private banks fell 82.1% → 82.2% → 82.2% → 81.7% over four months, while PSBs rose 17.8% → 17.7% → 17.6% → 18.1% — the largest single-month shift in this window. Private banks shifted from active to weakening; PSBs shifted from active to strengthening.
pos-category-share-scan.inference: PSBs gained 0.5 points of POS share in Apr — the largest single-month shift in this window — driven by government-backed financial inclusion mandates. Private banks are now 2.5 points below Aug's 84.2% peak, and the share will continue drifting down as PSBs expand terminals faster than private banks.

pos-private-share.title: Private bank POS share at 81.7% — down 0.5pp, lowest in 16 months
pos-private-share.observation: Private sector banks hold 81.74% of POS terminals in Apr 2026, down 0.51 points from Mar's 82.24% — the lowest level on record.
pos-private-share.direction: Share fell 82.1% → 82.2% → 82.2% → 81.7% over four months — the 0.5-point decline from Mar to Apr is the largest single-month drop in this window.
pos-private-share.inference: Private banks lost POS share in Apr after Mar's recovery — the 0.5-point decline confirms PSBs are adding terminals faster than private banks, driven by government-backed financial inclusion mandates. The share is now 2.5 points below Aug's 84.2% peak and will continue drifting down as PSBs expand aggressively.

pos-psb-share.title: PSB POS share at 18.1% — up 0.5pp, highest in 16 months
pos-psb-share.observation: Public sector banks hold 18.14% of POS terminals in Apr 2026, up 0.50 points from Mar's 17.63% — the highest level on record.
pos-psb-share.direction: Share rose 17.8% → 17.7% → 17.6% → 18.1% over four months — the 0.5-point gain from Mar to Apr is the largest single-month increase in this window.
pos-psb-share.inference: PSBs regained POS share in Apr after Mar's pullback — the 0.5-point gain confirms PSBs are expanding terminals faster than private banks, driven by government-backed financial inclusion mandates. The share is now at its highest level since Jan 2025 and will continue rising as PSBs prioritise merchant acceptance infrastructure.

pos-terminals-abs.title: POS terminals fell to 11.45M — 322K-unit drop erased recovery
pos-terminals-abs.observation: POS terminal count stands at 11,446,998 in Apr 2026, down 322,363 units from 11,769,361 in Mar 2026.
pos-terminals-abs.direction: Fell 11,769,361 → 11,446,998, erasing all gains from the four-month recovery that added 295K units from Dec's low of 11.47M — the network is now back below Jan's level of 11.55M.
pos-terminals-abs.inference: The recovery has collapsed. Apr's 322K-unit drop is the largest single-month decline since Nov's catastrophic 1.05M-unit fall, and it wipes out the entire Dec-Mar stabilisation. The network is now 862K units below Oct's peak of 12.31M and trending lower — this is not a correction, it is a structural contraction in physical acceptance infrastructure.

pos-terminals-pos-streak.title: POS terminal growth streak reset to zero — trend break confirmed
pos-terminals-pos-streak.observation: POS terminals have grown for 0 consecutive periods in Apr 2026, down from 4 periods in Mar 2026.
pos-terminals-pos-streak.direction: Streak reset from 4 periods to 0 — the four-month recovery run that began in Dec 2025 has ended, and the network is now contracting again.
pos-terminals-pos-streak.inference: The streak reset is the highest-signal moment in this dataset. The four-month stabilisation was not durable expansion — it was a pause before another leg down. The network has now contracted in 2 of the last 6 months, with both declines erasing prior gains. Physical POS infrastructure is in structural retreat.

pos-terminals-yoy.title: POS terminals at 1.5% YoY — weakest growth in this window
pos-terminals-yoy.observation: POS terminals grew 1.45% year-on-year in Apr 2026, down from 6.05% in Mar 2026.
pos-terminals-yoy.direction: YoY growth decelerated 6.0% → 1.5%, a 4.6-point drop — the rate has collapsed from Jan's 11.5% peak and is now at the floor of this 4-period series.
pos-terminals-yoy.inference: The 1.5% YoY rate represents just 163K net units added over 12 months, down from 670K in Mar. The deceleration is driven by both a rising base (Apr 2025's 11.28M) and Apr 2026's absolute decline. The network is barely growing on a year-on-year basis, and the trajectory is pointing toward zero or negative YoY by mid-2026 if the contraction continues.

upi-qr-abs.title: UPI QR codes hit 770.8M — record high, 9.6M-code gain
upi-qr-abs.observation: UPI QR code deployment reached 770,811,461 in Apr 2026, up 9,594,067 codes from 761,217,394 in Mar 2026.
upi-qr-abs.direction: Rose 761,217,394 → 770,811,461, accelerating from Mar's 7.0M-code gain — the 9.6M monthly increase is 37% larger and marks the strongest expansion since Feb's 15.8M-code surge.
upi-qr-abs.inference: UPI QR deployment has re-accelerated after Mar's slowdown, confirming the structural expansion is intact. The network is adding 9–10M codes per month, on track to exceed 780M by May 2026. The 9.6M gain in Apr is the second-largest in this 16-period series, and it comes as POS terminals contract — the bifurcation between digital and physical acceptance infrastructure is widening.

upi-qr-per-pos.title: UPI QR codes at 67.3× per POS — highest ratio on record
upi-qr-per-pos.observation: There are now 67.3 UPI QR codes for every POS terminal in Apr 2026, up 2.66 from 64.7 in Mar 2026.
upi-qr-per-pos.direction: Ratio surged 64.7× → 67.3×, the largest single-month jump in this 16-period series — the gap has widened by 5.5× since Nov's 61.8× low.
upi-qr-per-pos.inference: The bifurcation has resumed. UPI QR's 9.6M-code gain in Apr dwarfed POS's 322K-unit decline, driving the ratio to a new record high. For every POS terminal deployed, there are now 67 UPI QR codes — the structural shift toward lightweight digital acceptance is accelerating. Physical infrastructure is contracting while digital infrastructure scales exponentially.

upi-qr-yoy.title: UPI QR codes at 16.4% YoY — highest growth in this window
upi-qr-yoy.observation: UPI QR codes grew 16.37% year-on-year in Apr 2026, up from 15.70% in Mar 2026.
upi-qr-yoy.direction: YoY growth re-accelerated 15.7% → 16.4%, a 0.7-point gain — the rate has now stabilised in the 15–16% range across the 4-period series, with Apr at the top end.
upi-qr-yoy.inference: The 16.4% YoY rate represents 108M net codes added over 12 months, the highest absolute gain in this series. The re-acceleration from Mar's 15.7% confirms the structural expansion is intact despite the high base from Apr 2025 (662.4M codes). The network is adding 100M+ codes annually, with no signs of deceleration — UPI QR is the dominant acceptance infrastructure in India.

debit_card_txn.narrative: Debit card cash withdrawals at POS terminals are in terminal decline with no recovery signal. Apr 2026 value fell 11% month-on-month to ₹15.1M — the lowest since Aug 2025 and 45% below the Jan 2025 peak. Transaction volume hit a new all-time low of 8,094, down 67% from the Jan 2025 spike. The positive 6.0% YoY value growth is a pure base effect from the already-depressed Apr 2025 floor — not a recovery. The channel has never registered above 0.00% share of debit card value or volume, confirming it is economically irrelevant. Issuers should treat this feature as sunset-complete and redirect resources to UPI and card-present purchase flows immediately.

dc-atm-val-abs.title: ATM cash withdrawals ₹2.31L Cr — down ₹17,213 Cr from Mar
dc-atm-val-abs.observation: Debit card ATM cash withdrawals totaled ₹2,312,062,478 thousands (₹2.31 lakh crore) in Apr 2026.
dc-atm-val-abs.direction: Value fell ₹2.48L Cr → ₹2.31L Cr, dropping ₹17,213 crore — erasing the prior period's sharp recovery and falling below the 25th percentile for the first time in the 16-month window.
dc-atm-val-abs.inference: The Mar recovery was a false signal — cash withdrawal demand has collapsed back to the lower range. The ₹2.31L Cr level is the weakest reading on record. ATM network rationalization should resume; the structural decline thesis is back in force.

dc-atm-val-share.title: ATM cash share 86.2% — down 0.54pp, near floor
dc-atm-val-share.observation: ATM withdrawals accounted for 86.21% of total debit card transaction value in Apr 2026.
dc-atm-val-share.direction: Share fell 86.75% → 86.21%, dropping 0.54pp and moving below the 25th percentile to the lower range of the 16-month window.
dc-atm-val-share.inference: Cash is losing value share to digital channels at an accelerating pace. The 86.2% level is near the historical floor. Digital POS and ecommerce are structurally gaining share — the 87%+ equilibrium is broken.

dc-atm-val-yoy.title: ATM value -5.0% YoY — contraction moderating slightly
dc-atm-val-yoy.observation: ATM withdrawal value declined 5.05% year-on-year in Apr 2026.
dc-atm-val-yoy.direction: Contraction moderated -5.4% → -5.0% YoY, improving 0.33pp but remaining near the median of the 4-period window.
dc-atm-val-yoy.inference: The structural decline is intact but no longer accelerating. The -5.0% rate is marginally better than the -5.4% floor. If the next period improves further, the trend may be stabilizing — but the current rate still signals sustained cash withdrawal erosion.

dc-atm-vol-abs.title: ATM withdrawals 413M transactions — all-time low on record
dc-atm-vol-abs.observation: Debit card ATM withdrawals totaled 413,006,692 transactions in Apr 2026.
dc-atm-vol-abs.direction: Volume fell 444M → 413M transactions, dropping 31.0M — the sharpest monthly decline in the 16-month window and the lowest absolute level on record.
dc-atm-vol-abs.inference: The Mar recovery was a head-fake — visit frequency has collapsed to a new floor. The 413M level is 7% below the prior minimum. ATM network rationalization must accelerate; the structural collapse thesis is confirmed.

dc-atm-vol-share.title: ATM volume share 80.8% — down 0.58pp from Mar
dc-atm-vol-share.observation: ATM withdrawals represented 80.84% of total debit card transaction volume in Apr 2026.
dc-atm-vol-share.direction: Share fell 81.42% → 80.84%, dropping 0.58pp and returning to the median range of the 16-month window.
dc-atm-vol-share.inference: The 81.4% spike was a one-period outlier — ATM visit share is back to the 80%–81% equilibrium. Digital channels are not losing transaction count on a sustained basis. The 80.8% level is stable, not collapsing.

dc-atm-vol-yoy.title: ATM volume -9.8% YoY — contraction moderating slightly
dc-atm-vol-yoy.observation: ATM withdrawal volume declined 9.79% year-on-year in Apr 2026.
dc-atm-vol-yoy.direction: Contraction moderated -10.4% → -9.8% YoY, improving 0.65pp and moving to the median of the 4-period window.
dc-atm-vol-yoy.inference: Visit frequency contraction is easing marginally — the -9.8% rate is 0.65pp better than the prior period. The structural decline is intact but no longer accelerating. If the next period improves further, the trend may be stabilizing.

dc-ecom-val-abs.title: Ecommerce value ₹12,465 Cr — down ₹969 Cr from Mar
dc-ecom-val-abs.observation: Debit card ecommerce transactions totaled ₹124,651,148 thousands (₹12,465 crore) in Apr 2026.
dc-ecom-val-abs.direction: Value fell ₹13,434 Cr → ₹12,465 Cr, dropping ₹969 crore and returning to the median range of the 16-month window.
dc-ecom-val-abs.inference: The Mar surge was a one-period spike, not a breakout — ecommerce value is back to the ₹12,000–₹13,000 Cr oscillation range. The structural growth narrative is weakening. Digital commerce is not gaining sustained traction.

dc-ecom-val-share.title: Ecommerce value share 4.65% — holding upper range
dc-ecom-val-share.observation: Ecommerce transactions accounted for 4.65% of total debit card transaction value in Apr 2026.
dc-ecom-val-share.direction: Share fell 4.69% → 4.65%, dropping 0.04pp but remaining above the 75th percentile in the upper range of the 16-month window.
dc-ecom-val-share.inference: Digital commerce is holding value share near the historical upper range despite the absolute value decline. The 4.65% level is stable — ecommerce is oscillating in the 4.6%–4.7% band with no clear directional bias.

dc-ecom-val-yoy.title: Ecommerce value +5.8% YoY — fastest growth on record
dc-ecom-val-yoy.observation: Ecommerce transaction value grew 5.76% year-on-year in Apr 2026.
dc-ecom-val-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated +2.7% → +5.8% YoY, improving 3.03pp and reaching the highest level in the 4-period window.
dc-ecom-val-yoy.inference: The breakout is confirmed — ecommerce value is growing at the fastest rate on record. The structural contraction narrative is fully invalidated. Digital commerce is gaining sustained momentum; banks should prioritize ecommerce partnerships and digital channel investment.

dc-ecom-vol-abs.title: Ecommerce volume 25.7M transactions — down 0.6M from Mar
dc-ecom-vol-abs.observation: Debit card ecommerce transactions totaled 25,662,638 in Apr 2026.
dc-ecom-vol-abs.direction: Volume fell 26.3M → 25.7M transactions, dropping 0.6M and returning to the median range of the 16-month window.
dc-ecom-vol-abs.inference: The Mar recovery was a one-period spike — transaction count is back to the median range. Adoption is oscillating, not breaking out. Ecommerce volume is stable in the 25M–27M band with no clear directional trend.

dc-ecom-vol-share.title: Ecommerce volume share 5.02% — up 0.20pp, upper range
dc-ecom-vol-share.observation: Ecommerce transactions represented 5.02% of total debit card transaction volume in Apr 2026.
dc-ecom-vol-share.direction: Share rose 4.82% → 5.02%, gaining 0.20pp and moving above the 75th percentile to the upper range of the 16-month window.
dc-ecom-vol-share.inference: Volume share is breaking out — ecommerce is gaining transaction share at an accelerating pace. The 5.02% level is the highest in the 16-month window. Digital commerce is structurally gaining share; banks should expect ecommerce to hold above 5.0%.

dc-ecom-vol-yoy.title: Ecommerce volume -12.9% YoY — contraction moderating, best rate
dc-ecom-vol-yoy.observation: Ecommerce transaction volume declined 12.94% year-on-year in Apr 2026.
dc-ecom-vol-yoy.direction: Contraction moderated -14.1% → -12.9% YoY, improving 1.15pp and reaching the best level in the 4-period window.
dc-ecom-vol-yoy.inference: Visit frequency contraction is easing for the third consecutive period — the -12.9% decline is 1.15pp better than Mar. The structural collapse narrative is weakening. If the next period improves further, the trend break is confirmed and ecommerce adoption will return to growth.

dc-other-val-abs.title: DC other value flat at ₹168.2 Cr — upper range
dc-other-val-abs.observation: Debit card 'other' transactions totaled ₹1,682,311 thousands (₹168.2 crore) in Apr 2026.
dc-other-val-abs.direction: Value held nearly flat: ₹168.0 Cr → ₹168.2 Cr, up ₹0.2 crore and remaining above the 75th percentile of the 16-month range.
dc-other-val-abs.inference: The category has stabilised at the upper end of its historical range for two consecutive periods. However, absolute scale remains negligible (under 0.1% of total debit card value) — this is not a strategic channel for portfolio allocation.

dc-other-val-share.title: DC other share locked at 0.06% — no movement
dc-other-val-share.observation: Other transactions accounted for 0.06% of total debit card transaction value in Apr 2026.
dc-other-val-share.direction: Share held flat at 0.06%, unchanged for three consecutive periods and locked near the historical maximum.
dc-other-val-share.inference: No new use case is emerging. This category remains structurally irrelevant — banks should continue to exclude it from strategic planning and resource allocation.

dc-other-val-yoy.title: DC other value surged 20.7% YoY — highest on record
dc-other-val-yoy.observation: Other transaction value grew 20.73% year-on-year in Apr 2026.
dc-other-val-yoy.direction: YoY growth re-accelerated sharply: +5.5% → +20.7%, up 15.3 points and now at the maximum of the 4-period YoY window.
dc-other-val-yoy.inference: Growth has spiked to the highest rate on record, but the absolute base remains negligible (₹168 Cr). This volatility is noise — banks should not allocate resources to this segment despite the headline growth rate.

dc-other-vol-abs.title: DC other volume fell to 74,041 — below 25th percentile
dc-other-vol-abs.observation: Debit card 'other' transactions totaled 74,041 in Apr 2026.
dc-other-vol-abs.direction: Volume declined 78,017 → 74,041 transactions, down 3,976 and now sitting below the 25th percentile of the 16-month range.
dc-other-vol-abs.inference: Transaction count has reversed the Mar recovery and fallen to the lower end of the historical range. Absolute numbers remain too low to influence debit card strategy — banks should not allocate resources to this category.

dc-other-vol-share.title: DC other volume share flat at 0.01% — structurally capped
dc-other-vol-share.observation: Other transactions represented 0.01% of total debit card transaction volume in Apr 2026.
dc-other-vol-share.direction: Share held flat at 0.01% (rounded to 0.0%), unchanged for 16 consecutive periods and structurally capped near zero.
dc-other-vol-share.inference: Volume share remains structurally irrelevant. This category will not move the needle on debit card strategy — banks should ignore it for resource allocation.

dc-other-vol-yoy.title: DC other volume -12.9% YoY — decline moderating
dc-other-vol-yoy.observation: Other transaction volume contracted 12.87% year-on-year in Apr 2026.
dc-other-vol-yoy.direction: YoY decline moderated: -20.3% → -12.9%, improving 7.5 points and now at the maximum (least negative) of the 4-period YoY window.
dc-other-vol-yoy.inference: The category is shrinking less sharply than in prior periods, but remains in structural decline. No growth driver exists — banks should not allocate resources to this segment despite the moderating contraction.

dc-pos-val-abs.title: DC POS value flat at ₹243.4B — no momentum
dc-pos-val-abs.observation: Debit card POS transaction value totaled ₹243.4 billion in Apr 2026.
dc-pos-val-abs.direction: Value held nearly flat: ₹243.5B → ₹243.4B, down ₹30 million and sitting near the median of the 16-month range.
dc-pos-val-abs.inference: The Mar recovery has stalled — POS value is locked in the ₹240B–₹260B range with no breakout momentum. The channel has stabilised but is not growing. Banks should not expect POS to drive incremental debit card value in the near term.

dc-pos-val-share.title: DC POS share rose to 9.08% — highest in 6 months
dc-pos-val-share.observation: POS transactions accounted for 9.08% of total debit card transaction value in Apr 2026.
dc-pos-val-share.direction: Share rose from 8.50% in Mar 2026 to 9.08% in Apr 2026 — a 0.58 percentage point gain — now above the 75th percentile and at the highest level since Oct 2025.
dc-pos-val-share.inference: POS has regained structural share for the first time in six months, driven by relative strength as other channels (likely ATM cash) contracted. This is a meaningful shift — POS is reclaiming its footprint within the debit card mix despite flat absolute value.

dc-pos-val-yoy.title: DC POS value -7.0% YoY — contraction deepening
dc-pos-val-yoy.observation: POS transaction value contracted 7.04% year-on-year in Apr 2026.
dc-pos-val-yoy.direction: YoY growth deteriorated: -5.7% → -7.0%, down 1.3 points and now at the minimum (most negative) of the 4-period YoY window.
dc-pos-val-yoy.inference: The YoY decline has re-accelerated to the worst rate on record despite the share gain. The Apr 2025 base was stronger, exposing the structural weakness in current value. POS is losing ground year-on-year even as it gains share month-on-month — the overall debit card pool is contracting faster.

dc-pos-vol-abs.title: DC POS volume fell to 72.1M — below 25th percentile
dc-pos-vol-abs.observation: Debit card POS transaction volume totaled 72.1 million in Apr 2026.
dc-pos-vol-abs.direction: Volume declined from 74.9M in Mar 2026 to 72.1M in Apr 2026 — down 2.8M (3.7% month-on-month) — now below the 25th percentile and at the lowest level in the 16-month window.
dc-pos-vol-abs.inference: Transaction frequency has reversed the Mar recovery and fallen to a new low. Debit cardholders are using POS less frequently than at any point in this dataset. The channel is not stabilising — it is contracting to a new, lower equilibrium.

dc-pos-vol-share.title: DC POS volume share rose to 14.12% — partial recovery
dc-pos-vol-share.observation: POS transactions represented 14.12% of total debit card transaction volume in Apr 2026.
dc-pos-vol-share.direction: Share rose from 13.74% in Mar 2026 to 14.12% in Apr 2026 — a 0.38 percentage point gain — but remains below the 14.2%–14.9% band that held for seven months through Jan 2026.
dc-pos-vol-share.inference: POS has regained a sliver of frequency share for the second consecutive period, but is still at the bottom of its historical range. The channel is clawing back share as other channels contract, but has not returned to prior norms — it is stabilising at a structurally lower share of debit card transaction volume.

dc-pos-vol-yoy.title: DC POS volume -17.6% YoY — worst contraction on record
dc-pos-vol-yoy.observation: POS transaction volume contracted 17.57% year-on-year in Apr 2026.
dc-pos-vol-yoy.direction: YoY growth deteriorated: -16.4% → -17.6%, down 1.2 points and now at the minimum (most negative) of the 4-period YoY window.
dc-pos-vol-yoy.inference: The YoY decline has re-accelerated to the worst rate on record for the fourth consecutive period. POS transaction frequency is contracting at an accelerating pace — the channel is losing users, not just value per transaction. This is a structural decline, not a cyclical dip.

dc-pos-withdrawal-val-abs.title: DC POS cash withdrawals fell to ₹15.1M — lowest since Aug 2025
dc-pos-withdrawal-val-abs.observation: Debit card cash withdrawals at POS terminals totaled ₹15.1 million in Apr 2026.
dc-pos-withdrawal-val-abs.direction: Value dropped from ₹17.0M in Mar 2026 to ₹15.1M in Apr 2026 — down 11% month-on-month, erasing the prior period's stabilisation bounce and falling to the weakest level since Aug 2025.
dc-pos-withdrawal-val-abs.inference: The Mar 2026 recovery was a false signal. Apr 2026 confirms the channel is in accelerating decline, now 45% below the Jan 2025 peak of ₹27.7M. Issuers should treat this feature as sunset-complete and redirect resources to UPI and card-present purchase flows.

dc-pos-withdrawal-val-share.title: DC POS cash withdrawal share remains 0.00% — no economic footprint
dc-pos-withdrawal-val-share.observation: POS cash withdrawals accounted for 0.00% of total debit card transaction value in Apr 2026.
dc-pos-withdrawal-val-share.direction: Share held at 0.00% across all 16 periods, unchanged from Mar 2026.
dc-pos-withdrawal-val-share.inference: Despite the Apr 2026 value collapse, cash-at-POS has never registered in the debit card value mix. The feature is economically irrelevant — not a strategic consideration for issuers, acquirers, or network operators.

dc-pos-withdrawal-val-yoy.title: DC POS cash withdrawal value turned positive at 6.0% YoY — first growth in 4 periods
dc-pos-withdrawal-val-yoy.observation: POS cash withdrawal value grew 6.0% year-on-year in Apr 2026.
dc-pos-withdrawal-val-yoy.direction: YoY growth reversed from -17.9% in Mar 2026 to +6.0% in Apr 2026 — the first positive reading in the 4-period YoY window and the strongest comparison on record.
dc-pos-withdrawal-val-yoy.inference: The positive YoY is a pure base effect: Apr 2025 was the first post-spike month at ₹14.3M, already deeply depressed. The 6% growth is meaningless — Apr 2026 at ₹15.1M remains 45% below the Jan 2025 peak and in structural decline. Do not interpret this as recovery; the channel is terminal.

dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-abs.title: DC POS cash withdrawal volume hit 8,094 — new all-time low
dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-abs.observation: Debit card cash withdrawal transactions at POS terminals reached 8,094 in Apr 2026.
dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-abs.direction: Volume fell from 9,984 in Mar 2026 to 8,094 in Apr 2026 — down 19% month-on-month, breaking below the prior floor and now at the lowest level in the 16-period series.
dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-abs.inference: Transaction count is now 67% below the Jan 2025 peak of 24,327 and in freefall. The channel has no user base, no strategic value, and no path to recovery. Issuers should complete sunset immediately and reallocate infrastructure spend to UPI and card-present purchase acceptance.

dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-share.title: DC POS cash withdrawal volume share remains 0.00% — no transaction footprint
dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-share.observation: POS cash withdrawals represented 0.00% of total debit card transaction volume in Apr 2026.
dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-share.direction: Share held at 0.00% across all 16 periods, unchanged from Mar 2026.
dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-share.inference: Despite the 67% volume collapse from Jan 2025 to Apr 2026, cash-at-POS remains a rounding error in the debit card transaction mix. The feature has never scaled and shows no sign of registering in the overall volume distribution — not a material driver of debit card strategy.

dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-yoy.title: DC POS cash withdrawal volume contracted 44.4% YoY — decline moderating on low base
dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-yoy.observation: POS cash withdrawal transaction volume contracted 44.4% year-on-year in Apr 2026.
dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-yoy.direction: YoY decline improved from -47.7% in Mar 2026 to -44.4% in Apr 2026 — the second-least severe contraction in the 4-period YoY window, continuing the moderation trend from -49.2% in Jan 2026.
dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-yoy.inference: The year-on-year comparison is improving for the second time in three months, but only because the Apr 2025 base was already depressed at 14,548 transactions. Apr 2026 volume is down 44% versus prior year and 67% versus the Jan 2025 peak — moderation in the rate of decline does not signal recovery, merely a lower base effect as the spike rolls off. The channel is terminal.

credit_card_txn.narrative: Credit card transaction flows are bifurcating sharply. The 'other' category — likely tap-to-pay, transit, or merchant-initiated flows — continues its explosive ascent: ₹92,986 Cr value and 3.42M volume in Apr 2026, both all-time highs, with YoY growth still near 300–500% despite four months of deceleration. This new use case is embedding structurally, gaining share every month for a year straight, though it remains sub-1% of total flows. Meanwhile, POS — the traditional offline channel — is volatile and losing ground: Apr value fell 5.9% month-on-month to ₹73,849 Cr, erasing 42% of Mar's surge, while volume dropped 3.0% to 27.6 Cr. POS share has stayed below 50% for four straight months, confirming the 50/50 equilibrium is broken and ecommerce now dominates transaction counts. YoY growth rates tell the story: POS value at 8.8%, POS volume at 20.8%, both decelerating — while 'other' remains 5–6× faster. The strategic implication: offline retail is growing but structurally weaker; new digital-first use cases are the velocity play.

cc-atm-val-abs.title: CC ATM cash at ₹3.65L Cr — down ₹0.42L Cr from Mar
cc-atm-val-abs.observation: Credit card ATM cash withdrawals totalled ₹3.65L Cr in Apr 2026, down ₹0.42L Cr from Mar 2026.
cc-atm-val-abs.direction: Value fell ₹4.08L Cr → ₹3.65L Cr, a 10.3% drop that erases the entire Mar rebound and pushes the level back below the 16-period p25.
cc-atm-val-abs.inference: The Mar recovery was a one-month blip — ATM cash has collapsed back to its Feb trough, confirming the channel has no sustained demand and remains structurally weak.

cc-atm-val-share.title: ATM cash share at 0.18% — lowest in 16 months
cc-atm-val-share.observation: ATM cash withdrawals account for 0.18% of total credit card transaction value in Apr 2026, down 0.01pp from Mar 2026.
cc-atm-val-share.direction: Share slipped 0.19% → 0.18%, the first move below 0.19% in the series and now at the minimum of the 16-period range.
cc-atm-val-share.inference: ATM cash has lost another share point and is now at its lowest level on record — ecommerce and POS capture 99.82% of value, confirming this channel is economically irrelevant.

cc-atm-val-yoy.title: ATM cash value -1.95% YoY — contraction deepening
cc-atm-val-yoy.observation: Credit card ATM cash withdrawal value contracted 1.95% year-on-year in Apr 2026.
cc-atm-val-yoy.direction: YoY growth deteriorated -0.62% → -1.95%, reversing the Mar improvement and now back near the median of the -10.6% to -0.6% range.
cc-atm-val-yoy.inference: The moderation in ATM cash decline has reversed — the channel is contracting faster again, confirming the Mar slowdown was temporary and the structural decline is intact.

cc-atm-vol-abs.title: ATM withdrawals at 641K — down 66K from Mar
cc-atm-vol-abs.observation: Credit card ATM withdrawals totalled 641K transactions in Apr 2026, down 66K from Mar 2026.
cc-atm-vol-abs.direction: Volume fell 708K → 641K, a 9.4% drop that erases the entire Mar rebound and pushes the level back below the 16-period p25.
cc-atm-vol-abs.inference: Transaction count has collapsed back to its Feb trough — the Mar recovery was a one-month anomaly, and the channel remains structurally weak with no sustained demand.

cc-atm-vol-share.title: ATM volume share at 0.11% — all-time low
cc-atm-vol-share.observation: ATM cash withdrawals represent 0.11% of total credit card transaction volume in Apr 2026, down 0.01pp from Mar 2026.
cc-atm-vol-share.direction: Share fell 0.12% → 0.11%, the first move below 0.12% in the series and now at the minimum of the 16-period range.
cc-atm-vol-share.inference: ATM cash has hit a new all-time low in transaction frequency share — digital payments continue to outpace cash withdrawals, confirming the channel's structural irrelevance.

cc-atm-vol-yoy.title: ATM volume -6.55% YoY — decline re-accelerating
cc-atm-vol-yoy.observation: Credit card ATM withdrawal volume contracted 6.55% year-on-year in Apr 2026.
cc-atm-vol-yoy.direction: YoY growth deteriorated -4.77% → -6.55%, reversing the Mar improvement and now back near the median of the -10.6% to -4.8% range.
cc-atm-vol-yoy.inference: ATM cash transaction frequency is collapsing faster again — the Mar moderation was temporary, and the channel is back to shrinking at a 6.6% annual rate with no recovery signal.

cc-ecom-val-abs.title: Ecommerce at ₹123.4L Cr — down ₹17.6L Cr from Mar peak
cc-ecom-val-abs.observation: Credit card ecommerce transaction value totalled ₹123.4L Cr in Apr 2026, down ₹17.6L Cr from Mar 2026.
cc-ecom-val-abs.direction: Value fell ₹140.9L Cr → ₹123.4L Cr, a 12.5% drop that erases the Mar record and pushes the level back near the 16-period median.
cc-ecom-val-abs.inference: The Mar breakout was a one-month spike — ecommerce has retreated sharply but remains above the Oct–Feb trough, confirming the channel is still in expansion but with high month-to-month volatility.

cc-ecom-val-share.title: Ecommerce share at 62.41% — down 1.68pp from Mar
cc-ecom-val-share.observation: Ecommerce accounts for 62.41% of total credit card transaction value in Apr 2026, down 1.68pp from Mar 2026.
cc-ecom-val-share.direction: Share fell 64.09% → 62.41%, the largest single-month decline in the 16-period series and now back near the median of the 61.7–63.6% range.
cc-ecom-val-share.inference: Ecommerce has lost its Mar surge in value share — the channel is back to its 62% structural baseline, confirming the Mar spike was seasonal and POS is reclaiming share.

cc-ecom-val-yoy.title: Ecommerce value +6.05% YoY — growth decelerating
cc-ecom-val-yoy.observation: Credit card ecommerce transaction value grew 6.05% year-on-year in Apr 2026.
cc-ecom-val-yoy.direction: YoY growth decelerated 8.41% → 6.05%, a 2.35pp slowdown that moves the reading back near the median of the 4.5–8.4% range.
cc-ecom-val-yoy.inference: Ecommerce value growth has slowed sharply from its Mar peak — the channel is still expanding year-on-year but at a decelerating rate, signalling the Mar acceleration was temporary.

cc-ecom-vol-abs.title: Ecommerce volume at 280.6M — down 9.5M from Mar record
cc-ecom-vol-abs.observation: Credit card ecommerce transaction volume reached 280.6M in Apr 2026, down 9.5M from Mar 2026.
cc-ecom-vol-abs.direction: Volume fell 290.1M → 280.6M, a 3.3% drop from the all-time high but still above the 16-period p75 and 4.2% above the Jan 2026 prior peak.
cc-ecom-vol-abs.inference: Transaction count has retreated from its Mar record but remains near the upper end of the historical range — the channel is sustaining elevated frequency despite the Apr pullback, confirming structural demand is intact.

cc-ecom-vol-share.title: Ecommerce volume share at 50.09% — holding above 50%
cc-ecom-vol-share.observation: Ecommerce represents 50.09% of total credit card transaction volume in Apr 2026, down 0.09pp from Mar 2026.
cc-ecom-vol-share.direction: Share slipped 50.17% → 50.09%, a minimal decline that keeps the channel above 50% for the third consecutive month and at the upper end of the 16-period range.
cc-ecom-vol-share.inference: Ecommerce has consolidated its majority share of transaction frequency — the channel is sustaining dominance above 50% despite the Apr value retreat, confirming the shift to online is structural.

cc-ecom-vol-yoy.title: Ecommerce volume +26.82% YoY — growth holding steady
cc-ecom-vol-yoy.observation: Credit card ecommerce transaction volume grew 26.82% year-on-year in Apr 2026.
cc-ecom-vol-yoy.direction: YoY growth decelerated 27.48% → 26.82%, a minimal 0.66pp slowdown that keeps the reading near the median of the 26.1–27.5% range.
cc-ecom-vol-yoy.inference: Ecommerce transaction frequency is growing at 27% YoY with minimal deceleration — the channel is adding volume at 4.4× the value growth rate, confirming cardholders are transacting more often at lower ticket sizes.

cc-other-val-abs.title: CC other transactions hit ₹92,986 Cr — new record
cc-other-val-abs.observation: Credit card 'other' transaction value reached ₹92,986 Cr in Apr 2026, up ₹2,758 Cr from Mar 2026.
cc-other-val-abs.direction: Value rose ₹90,228 Cr → ₹92,986 Cr, the fourth consecutive monthly gain and the highest level on record — now 6× the Apr 2025 low of ₹15,687 Cr.
cc-other-val-abs.inference: The Mar recovery has extended into Apr with sustained momentum — this new transaction category (likely tap-to-pay, transit, or merchant-initiated flows) is still scaling rapidly despite the maturing base. The unbroken upward trajectory confirms structural embedding, not a ceiling.

cc-other-val-share.title: CC other share rises to 0.05% — highest on record
cc-other-val-share.observation: Other transactions account for 0.05% of total credit card transaction value in Apr 2026, up 0.01 points from Mar 2026.
cc-other-val-share.direction: Share rose 0.04% → 0.05%, the highest reading in the 16-period window and the first time above 0.04%.
cc-other-val-share.inference: Despite explosive absolute growth, other channels remain strategically irrelevant in value terms — ecommerce and POS continue to dominate credit card spending by orders of magnitude. This category is not yet material for product strategy or revenue forecasting.

cc-other-val-yoy.title: CC other value growth 492.8% YoY — still extreme
cc-other-val-yoy.observation: Credit card 'other' transaction value grew 492.8% YoY in Apr 2026, down 3.1 points from Mar 2026's 495.9%.
cc-other-val-yoy.direction: YoY growth decelerated 495.9% → 492.8%, the fourth consecutive monthly slowdown and now at the floor of the four-period range.
cc-other-val-yoy.inference: This remains the fastest-growing credit card channel by a wide margin — a 5× year-on-year surge confirms a new use case is scaling rapidly. The deceleration is expected as the base effect fades, but the absolute rate is still extreme. Monitor whether this velocity sustains below 450% in coming quarters as the category matures.

cc-other-vol-abs.title: CC other volume hits 3.42M — new all-time high
cc-other-vol-abs.observation: Credit card 'other' transaction volume reached 3.42M in Apr 2026, up 72K from Mar 2026.
cc-other-vol-abs.direction: Volume rose 3.34M → 3.42M, the fourth consecutive monthly gain and the highest level on record — now 4× the Apr 2025 low of 854K.
cc-other-vol-abs.inference: The Mar recovery has extended into Apr with sustained momentum — this new transaction type (likely tap-to-pay, transit, or subscriptions) is still embedding rapidly in cardholder behaviour despite the maturing base. The unbroken upward trajectory confirms structural adoption, not a ceiling.

cc-other-vol-share.title: CC other volume share 0.61% — twelfth straight gain
cc-other-vol-share.observation: Other transactions represent 0.61% of total credit card transaction volume in Apr 2026, up 0.03 points from Mar 2026.
cc-other-vol-share.direction: Share rose 0.58% → 0.61%, the highest reading in the 16-period window and the twelfth consecutive monthly gain.
cc-other-vol-share.inference: Other channels are gaining share for the twelfth straight month — this confirms the category is still embedding structurally in cardholder behaviour even as YoY growth decelerates. The share trajectory is decoupling from the absolute level, signaling this use case is becoming a permanent fixture in the transaction mix.

cc-other-vol-yoy.title: CC other volume growth 299.8% YoY — fourth deceleration
cc-other-vol-yoy.observation: Credit card 'other' transaction volume grew 299.8% YoY in Apr 2026, down 8.6 points from Mar 2026's 308.4%.
cc-other-vol-yoy.direction: YoY growth decelerated 308.4% → 299.8%, the fourth consecutive monthly slowdown and now at the floor of the four-period range.
cc-other-vol-yoy.inference: This remains the fastest-growing credit card channel by volume — a 4× year-on-year surge confirms a new use case is scaling rapidly. The deceleration is expected as the base effect fades, but the absolute rate is still extreme. Monitor whether this velocity sustains below 300% in coming quarters as the category matures.

cc-pos-val-abs.title: CC POS value falls to ₹73,849 Cr — Mar surge reversed
cc-pos-val-abs.observation: Credit card POS transaction value fell to ₹73,849 Cr in Apr 2026, down ₹4,622 Cr from Mar 2026's ₹78,470 Cr.
cc-pos-val-abs.direction: Value dropped ₹78,470 Cr → ₹73,849 Cr, a 5.9% monthly decline — erasing 42% of Mar's ₹11,035 Cr surge and falling back near the Feb level of ₹67,435 Cr.
cc-pos-val-abs.inference: The Mar POS surge was a one-month spike, not a sustained recovery — Apr's pullback confirms offline retail demand remains volatile and has not stabilised near festive-season levels. The anticipated normalisation near ₹78,000 Cr has failed. Lenders should expect continued month-to-month swings as consumer spending patterns remain uneven.

cc-pos-val-share.title: CC POS value share rises to 37.4% — reverses decline
cc-pos-val-share.observation: POS transactions captured 37.4% of total credit card value in Apr 2026, up 1.7 points from Mar 2026's 35.7%.
cc-pos-val-share.direction: Share rose 35.7% → 37.4%, reversing the five-month decline and posting the largest single-month gain in this dataset.
cc-pos-val-share.inference: The structural share loss to ecommerce has paused — Apr's 1.7 point gain suggests offline retail recaptured wallet share despite falling absolute value, meaning ecommerce contracted even more sharply. This is a tactical reversal, not a trend break. The sub-40% share level confirms POS remains structurally weaker than the Nov 2025 peak of 40.0%.

cc-pos-val-yoy.title: CC POS value growth 8.8% YoY — decelerates again
cc-pos-val-yoy.observation: Credit card POS transaction value grew 8.8% YoY in Apr 2026, down 1.0 points from Mar 2026's 9.8%.
cc-pos-val-yoy.direction: YoY growth decelerated 9.8% → 8.8%, falling back near the Feb floor of 8.5% after Mar's brief re-acceleration.
cc-pos-val-yoy.inference: The Mar re-acceleration was a one-month blip — Apr's 8.8% YoY confirms POS value growth has settled near the bottom of the 8.5–9.8% range. Offline retail is growing steadily but remains slower than ecommerce, which is why POS share continues to fluctuate below 40% despite positive absolute growth.

cc-pos-vol-abs.title: CC POS volume falls to 27.6 Cr — Mar record reversed
cc-pos-vol-abs.observation: Credit card POS transaction volume fell to 27.6 Cr in Apr 2026, down 845K from Mar 2026's 28.4 Cr.
cc-pos-vol-abs.direction: Volume dropped 28.4 Cr → 27.6 Cr, a 3.0% monthly decline — erasing 21% of Mar's 4.1 Cr surge and falling back below the Dec 2025 peak of 27.3 Cr.
cc-pos-vol-abs.inference: The Mar all-time high was a one-month spike, not a sustained breakout — Apr's pullback confirms offline retail transaction counts remain volatile and have not stabilised above festive-season levels. The anticipated sustained consumer engagement at physical points of sale has not materialised. Lenders should expect continued month-to-month swings.

cc-pos-vol-share.title: CC POS volume share 49.2% — still below 50%
cc-pos-vol-share.observation: POS transactions accounted for 49.2% of total credit card transaction volume in Apr 2026, up 0.1 points from Mar 2026's 49.1%.
cc-pos-vol-share.direction: Share rose 49.1% → 49.2%, the first monthly gain since Jan 2026 but still the fourth consecutive month below 50% and near the record low.
cc-pos-vol-share.inference: The 50/50 channel equilibrium remains broken — POS has now stayed below majority share for four straight months despite the Apr uptick. The new structural reality is a sub-50% POS share, confirming online channels are growing faster in transaction count terms. Lenders should plan for a gradual shift toward online-dominant channel mix.

cc-pos-vol-yoy.title: CC POS volume growth 20.8% YoY — decelerates to floor
cc-pos-vol-yoy.observation: Credit card POS transaction volume grew 20.8% YoY in Apr 2026, down 2.6 points from Mar 2026's 23.4%.
cc-pos-vol-yoy.direction: YoY growth decelerated 23.4% → 20.8%, falling to the floor of the four-period range after Mar's brief re-acceleration.
cc-pos-vol-yoy.inference: The Mar re-acceleration was a one-month blip — Apr's 20.8% YoY confirms POS volume growth has settled near the bottom of the 20.8–23.4% range. Offline retail transaction counts are expanding robustly, but the simultaneous share loss to ecommerce signals online channels are growing even faster, likely above 22% YoY.
